topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in Sarah’s inbox like a physical blow. "Effective next Monday, you will be mentoring Alex on all operational procedures." Sarah stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat. Alex. The new hire. It felt like the floor had dropped out from under her. Her hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic of her keyboard digging into her palms. She reread the instructions, the jargon blurring into meaningless shapes. *Mentor? Me?* A cold knot tightened in her stomach, squeezing the air from her lungs. She tried to focus on the spreadsheet open on her monitor, but the numbers swam. Every muscle in her body felt suddenly rigid, a wire pulled too taut. She needed to find the right words, the right sequence of steps, but her mind was a blank, echoing chamber.

[story 2]

"So, you'll be taking over the training for Mark?" The manager's voice was cheerful, entirely unconcerned with the sheer weight of the task being assigned. Mark, fresh out of school, looked eager. Sarah swallowed hard, the dryness in her mouth making the action difficult. Her gaze darted around the open-plan office, taking in the bustling activity as if searching for an escape route. A flush crept up her neck, warm and undeniable. Her fingers, resting on the edge of the desk, started tapping an uneven rhythm against the wood. *This is too much. I don't know anything comprehensive enough.* A wave of sheer overwhelm washed over her, making it hard to draw a full breath. She mumbled something indistinct, needing to retreat before she said something entirely wrong.

[story 3]

When David announced the assignment, the fluorescent lights above seemed to flicker, magnifying every shadow in the room. David, bless his oblivious heart, just beamed, completely missing the stark white pallor of Sarah’s skin. "It's a great opportunity for you, Sarah! You know the system inside and out." Sarah felt a sudden, intense pressure building behind her eyes. She gripped the edge of the conference table until her knuckles were white, the pressure a desperate attempt to anchor herself to reality. Her internal monologue was a frantic scramble—*What if I forget something crucial? What if they ask the wrong question?* Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic drumbeat she was acutely aware of, loud enough, she was sure, for everyone to hear. She managed a tight, shaky nod, trying desperately to project calm she absolutely did not feel.
